focus: Resolve seam continuity with minimal edits.
next_head_excerpt:
# Chapter 5

Hours later, South St. Louis came on in low brick and sodium light as Eli crossed the river. The patched radiator had started its thin kettle whine again, a high note under the engine that he could feel through the steering wheel. The sedan smelled like old coffee and the sweet chemical edge of coolant. He drove with both windows cracked because he did not trust the gauge and because the air inside the car had turned stale with his own shirt and fear.

Theo's block sat under a row of flat-roof brick buildings with narrow porches and dented mailboxes bolted crooked at shoulder height. Somebody had parked a rusting grill half on the sidewalk, half in the dirt strip where grass used to be. A television thumped through an open downstairs window in quick bursts of crowd noise and ad jingles. Eli rolled past once, then circled again because he had missed the number and because he had still not decided what words he was going to use.

He parked in front of a fire hydrant anyway. He told himself it was two minutes. He shut the engine off and sat with his hands on the wheel while the metal clicked and cooled. Nora's message thread was open on his burner. The last text from her had come at three in the afternoon.

`You do not get to disappear and then call this confusion. Return funds today.`

He could have answered. He had answered versions of that message in his head for two days, each version sounding calm and practical until he looked at the numbers in his green folder and watched calm collapse into arithmetic. Mortgage arrears. June's tuition bill. The transfer he had made from emergency savings before he left, fourteen thousand dollars moved in twelve taps while Nora was on an overnight ICU shift and June was packing dish soap and twin XL sheets.

He locked the...
prev_tail_excerpt:
...er two, he drafted another text to June and erased it before the first line finished. An hour later he got up, sat at the tiny laminate desk under the mirror, and wrote figures on the back of a diner receipt.

Tow shortfall: paid. Repair total: four forty-five. Deposit on file with Darlene from Cora: one thirty-seven. Remaining repair balance: three oh eight if Darlene kept her estimate. Room: thirty-five. Fuel to St. Louis plus food: maybe one forty if he drove gentle and skipped meals that were not free. Cash on hand after room: two dollars plus loose change.

The numbers were clean. His life was not.

The bedside clock crawled to 4:10 before he opened Nora's thread one last time and sent: `I left after move-in. I took $14,000 from our emergency account. That's mortgage money and June's tuition, and I left you to carry it. I'm at Yard Bird by Pike Yard in Indiana with a dead radiator. I'll call at six and answer whatever you ask.`

The phone slammed against his palm with an incoming call from Nora before he could close the flip.

He watched it ring out, throat tight, then set it on his chest and listened to the interstate grind toward morning.

Outside, dawn thinned the black above the tow-yard fence. Five-oh-eight found him in bay two while Darlene wiped her hands on a rag and nodded at the driver's seat. She had tightened the lower hose clamp and packed a hairline leak with sealant the color of silver toothpaste. "Crank it."

He turned the key. The engine coughed once, shuddered hard enough to rattle the mirror, then caught and held a rough idle. A single green drop gathered at the lower hose and fell to the concrete.

"It runs," Darlene said. "It's not healed. Keep it under sixty, watch the gauge like it's your pulse, and don't pretend this bought you forgiveness."
